Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty flexible, maybe too flexible at times. I'd usually set aside a few hours in the evening when I could listen to lecture recordings or podcasts related to the course material since I learn best by hearing things. With about 12 hours a week dedicated to studying across 8 online courses, I had to spread myself thin, but I tried to at least touch base with each course regularly. I found that having background audio while doing other tasks helped me absorb information better.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurricular commitments that sometimes ate into my study time, so I'd have to adjust accordingly. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying at the same times each evening, but other weeks everything would shift based on deadlines. I tried to maintain some structure, but life just doesn't always cooperate with ideal schedules.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment reminder would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about new content or a discussion post, and that would pull me back into the LMS. I also logged in when I needed to check my grades or see if there was feedback on something I submitted. It wasn't always intrinsic motivation driving me there, if I'm being honest—more like external prompts keeping me on track.
Q4: After a few weeks, logging in became somewhat automatic, especially in the mornings when I'd check what was due that week. The mindfulness practice I started doing actually helped me establish better routines overall, including checking the LMS more regularly. Once I set up notifications on my phone, it became easier to make it a habit. It wasn't a deep engagement every time, but at least I was staying aware of what was happening in the course.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over 150 minutes of video content because hearing the explanations really helped concepts stick in my mind. The discussion forums were also useful when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of—reading those exchanges gave me different perspectives. Anything I could listen to while multitasking felt particularly valuable to me.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped the reading materials, especially long PDFs or textbook chapters. My brain just doesn't absorb written content as well, so I'd skim them at best or ignore them entirely. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant if the video already covered the same material. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions—only had two throughout the course—because I felt awkward reaching out and my schedule didn't align well with others.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be assessed or if it genuinely helped me understand a difficult concept. When I could see the practical application of something, I was more motivated to engage with it. Also, if the activity was audio-based or could be consumed passively while I did other things, it felt more efficient. Anything that felt like busywork without clear learning outcomes just frustrated me.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd cut when I was stressed or short on time. Required activities had this weight to them, like I knew they'd affect my grade, so I prioritized them even when my motivation was low. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and then later never came. It's not that I didn't see their value, but when you're juggling 8 courses, you have to make choices.
Q9: Oh absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my commute or while exercising. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos that explained concepts in different ways. Sometimes I'd record myself summarizing key points and play those back later—that really helped with retention. The LMS would have no idea about any of that learning happening.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely but the LMS wouldn't reflect it at all. For instance, I'd download a week's worth of materials in one sitting, then spend days working through them offline. Or I'd be discussing course concepts with a friend over coffee, working through problems together. My exam preparation was mostly done with printed notes and audio recordings I made myself, none of which showed up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue studying from those files for days or weeks. The videos especially—I'd download them and watch them multiple times offline, rewinding parts I didn't understand. My LMS activity would show one view, but in reality, I might have watched something three or four times. It was just more convenient to have everything saved locally.
Q12: Mid-semester was really tough when several deadlines converged across my courses and my stress levels shot up. Even though I'm generally resilient, there were moments when I just felt overwhelmed and disconnected from the material. My attendance dropped significantly during those periods—I know my attendance rate isn't great at 43%. The mindfulness exercises helped me push through eventually, but those low points were real.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just enough to stay afloat. Confusion about assignment requirements sometimes made me avoid starting tasks because I didn't know where to begin. High stress paradoxically made me less productive even when I spent time on the platform. I'd find myself logged in but just staring at the screen, not really processing anything.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things or just zoning out. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. There were times I'd complete activities mechanically to check them off my list without actually learning anything. The task completion showed 81%, but the meaningful engagement was probably lower.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments that felt overwhelming or unclear, even when I knew they were important. My assignment completion rate of around 72% reflects that—I'd sometimes just not submit things if I felt I couldn't do them well. Procrastination was worst when my motivation was low and stress was high, which happened more than I'd like. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and sometimes tomorrow never came for certain tasks.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator, honestly. I'd have a burst of LMS activity in the days leading up to a due date, then things would go quiet again. My login patterns were very much tied to the assessment calendar. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged as consistently as I did. It's not ideal, but that external pressure was sometimes the only thing that got me moving when my motivation was low.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it gave me a little boost and made me want to engage more. But when feedback was critical or my scores were lower than expected, I'd sometimes avoid the course for a bit because it felt discouraging. I tried to use feedback constructively, but with my moderate anxiety levels, sometimes it just made me more stressed. The response time of about two hours for my submissions felt reasonable though.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on how I participated. When modules were clearly organized and I knew exactly what was expected, I engaged more consistently. But when things felt scattered or the path forward was unclear, I'd disengage. Having 8 online courses meant I gravitated toward the ones with clearer structures. This course was somewhere in the middle—not the most organized, but manageable enough that I could navigate it.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents I had to read carefully. The discussion forum requirements felt a bit forced sometimes—I posted 9 times, but not all of those were genuine engagement. Having flexibility in when I could access materials was crucial given my schedule and extracurriculars.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure out what they need to do or is confused about requirements. They might be clicking around, watching videos, reading instructions, but not feeling confident enough to actually submit work. It could also be someone dealing with perfectionism or anxiety—they're engaging with the content but can't bring themselves to turn in something that feels incomplete. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggling, not thriving.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. My attendance was only 43% and my LMS activity was moderate, but I still passed with decent exam scores because a lot of my learning happened offline. Students who are efficient or who learn well through methods the LMS doesn't track can absolutely perform well without high visible activity. It doesn't mean they're not engaged—they're just engaged in ways the system can't see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns pretty accurately—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. They're good at capturing deadline-driven behavior and basic participation metrics. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, mental state while using the platform, and learning that happens through conversation or personal reflection. They show the what and when, but not the why or how well.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to dig deeper, or when I'm actively connecting new information to things I already know. True engagement feels different from compliance—it's intrinsic rather than just responding to external pressures. I wasn't always there this semester, but I had moments of genuine engagement.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my stress and stay resilient when things got tough. It wouldn't capture the conversations I had with friends about course material or the audio recordings I made for myself. It also wouldn't show that despite low motivation and high stress, I was genuinely trying my best given everything else going on in my life. The emotional journey of getting through this course is completely invisible in the data.
